chunk_summary: Tele-nutrition workflows for infant feeding and dietary issues emphasize structured diet recalls, photo audits, and short-term behavior experiments to improve adherence and outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of structured diet recalls and photo audits in tele-nutrition workflows.
Design one-week behavior change experiments with measurable outcomes for dietary improvement.
Identify practical strategies to improve adherence in families managing infant feeding and common dietary problems.
key_facts: Structured weekday-plus-weekend diet recalls improve data quality compared to open-ended conversations.
Photo audits include before-and-after meal photos, snack cupboard photos, and grocery list reviews.
Behavior change experiments should be short (one week) and include measurable goals, such as replacing one sugar-sweetened beverage daily with water or unsweetened milk.
Overly complex logs reduce adherence and increase dropout rates in tele-nutrition programs.
Follow-up messages should include specific goals, observation requests (e.g., photos or checklists), and fixed follow-up dates.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Treatment of Children and Adolescents With Obesity (AAP): Structured approaches improve adherence and outcomes in dietary interventions.
document_key_topics: Structured diet recalls and photo audits
chunk_heading: DIGITAL TOOLS & TELE-NUTRITION;TELE-COUNSELLING WORKFLOWS FOR INFANT FEEDING AND COMMON DIETARY PROBLEMS;REMOTE DIET RECALLS, PHOTO AUDITS, AND BEHAVIOR CHANGE EXPERIMENT DESIGN;
